content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Parker Byrd on UnsplashThree months ago I made a decision that seemed obvious at the time.
I would use AI coding tools for every single task. No exceptions. Claude Code for architecture decisions. GPT-5.6 for debugging. Cursor for writing functions. If an AI tool could touch it, I would let it.
The productivity gains were real. In the first two weeks I was shipping faster than I ever had. Pull requests that used to take three hours were done in forty minutes. My tech lead noticed. My sprint velocity numbers looked great on paper.
Then something started to go wrong. And it took me six weeks to figure out what it actually was.
The First Warning Sign I Ignored
It started with a bug that took me four hours to fix.
That is not unusual on its own. Complex bugs take time. But this one was different. The bug was in code I had written myself two months earlier, before I started the AI experiment. Code I understood completely when I wrote it.
Sitting there staring at it in July, I could not read it.
Not because it was complex. Because I had spent sixty days letting AI write my code and review my code and explain my code back to me, and somewhere in that…
